Alright, let's get in the flow. These are Google Ad flow charts. You've got two of them. The first one is your ad campaign structure, and then your next one is your Google advertising strategy. So let's look at your campaign structure first. So I know we've reiterated this a lot, but just remembering that really Google ads has got three levels, the highest level campaigns, and ad groups, and then ads. So while you're setting up your advertisements, you've got a couple different ways that you can go about doing it. So standardly, what most people do is they set up a campaign, so like a high level, let's say this campaign would be for a particular product line or for a particular promotion, and then underneath that campaign, they've got various ad groups. So this ad group could be targeting men, this one could be targeting women, this one could be targeting older people, this one could be targeting people who are already customers. So remembering that ad groups are basically the targeting. So and then below the ad groups, you can have as many ads as you'd like. And you can actually have the same, for example, if you're running video ads, you could have the same video ad here in group A as in group B as well. So you've got some flexibility there. Now, there is another way to go about setting up your ads. And this is a little bit more advanced. You know, it's a little less automated. And what I mean by that is, since your budget is set up here at your campaign level, with the advanced setup, what you're doing is you're making one campaign for one ad group for one ad. So basically, every time you want to test a different audience, or every time you want to test a different ad to a different audience, or any sort of, you know, anything that's different, you're going to create a new campaign every time. So as you can imagine, this could lead to having a lot of campaigns. Now, why would you want to do this? Well, here in this standard setup, the way that it works is you're going to set your budget at your campaign level. So let's just say, Hey, I want to spend a total of $1,000. And then what you're doing is you're saying, Google, you go ahead and you decide, you know, which target audience is better to spend the money on. And then within those, those target, those target ad groups, you know, go ahead, Google and decide which ad to run. Now, the general thought process is that Google wants you to succeed, they want your ads to do well. Now, this can work out the best. And the reason that this is good a lot of times for beginners is because of the fact that, you know, it's a little less work, it's more automated. But just know that if you're, you know, if your your search terms are about dog treats, and your landing page are about dog collars, it's not going to work out too well. So the more that you can match the actual landing page to your term, it's not only going to convert better, but it's going to give your your search ads a higher quality score, meaning that Google goes, Oh, okay, well, these ads match these landing pages, we like that, that's good, let's give them a good quality score and therefore, give them a better deal on advertising. So anytime that you can do a good thing for the user experience, Google is going to cut you a break on your cost.
